hypertext 1.2.0
  • Reference

  • svg

building components

Source: vignettes/articles/components.Rmd
components.Rmd
library(hypertext)

the core idea in hypertext is simple: tag functions are just R functions, and R functions compose naturally. no need for a framework or a special component system — a component is just a function that returns a hypertext.tag.

with hypertext, you can use any css framework of your choice. but for the examples that follow we’ll use bootstrap classes for demo purposes and because this site is built using pkgdown which uses bootstrap by default.

basic component

the simplest component is a function that wraps a tag with a fixed structure, exposing only the parts that change as arguments.

a really good example here is a button:

btn <- tags$button(
  type = "button",
  class = "btn",
  "Click Me!"
)

in your app and depending on your styling, this snippet might look like this:

cat(
  render(btn)
)

if you want to re-use this snippet in other parts of your app, you need to convert it to a component:

Button <- function() {
  tags$button(
    type = "button",
    class = "btn",
    "Click Me!"
  )
}

cat(
  render(Button())
)

that’d be the default button rendered. but sometimes, you need to tweak the button a little bit. so let’s identify the moving parts:

  • type: As per mdn docs, the type can either be:
    • “submit”: the button submits form data to the server.
    • “reset”: the button resets all the controls to their initial values.
    • “button”: the button has no default behaviour, and does nothing when pressed by default.
  • class: we’d need to pass more [custom] classes to our button. these extra classes would dictate it’s styling and behaviour when clicked.
  • label: each button in our app probably has a different label.

with that, let’s now improve the Button() component:

#' A Button
#'
#' @param label String /// Required.
#'              The button label.
#'
#' @param type String /// Optional.
#'             Button type. Either:
#'             - "button" (default)
#'             - "submit"
#'             - "reset"
#'
#' @param class Character vector /// Optional.
#'              CSS classes to apply to the button.
#'
#' @return `hypertext.tag`
#'
#' @export
Button <- function(
  label,
  type = c("button", "submit", "reset"),
  class = NULL
) {
  type <- match.arg(arg = type)

  tags$button(
    type = type,
    class = c("btn", class),
    label
  )
}

see how this works? we even added some function docs there. practically, you’d call the component like this:

btn_a <- Button(label = "Default")

btn_b <- Button(
  label = "Submit",
  type = "submit",
  class = "btn-primary"
)

btn_c <- Button(
  label = "Confirm",
  class = "btn-success"
)

btn_d <- Button(
  label = "Delete",
  class = "btn-danger"
)

and this how those would look:

my_btns <- tag_list(
  btn_a,
  btn_b,
  btn_c,
  btn_d
)

cat(
  render(my_btns)
)

composition

say we have this Badge() component:

#' A Badge
#'
#' @param label String /// Required.
#'              Label to show on the badge.
#'
#' @param variant String /// Optional.
#'                Utility class. Must be one of:
#'                - "primary" (default)
#'                - "success"
#'                - "danger"
#'
#' @return `hypertext.tag`
#'
#' @export
Badge <- function(
  label,
  variant = c("primary", "success", "danger")
) {
  variant <- match.arg(arg = variant)

  tags$span(
    class = c(
      "badge",
      paste0("text-bg-", variant)
    ),
    label
  )
}

badge_a <- Badge("new")
badge_b <- Badge("deprecated", variant = "danger")
badge_c <- Badge("stable", variant = "success")

the variant argument maps directly to a bootstrap utility class. the caller controls the label and colour; the structure is fixed inside the function.

cat(
  render(badge_a),
  render(badge_b),
  render(badge_c)
)

new deprecated stable

components can call other components.

an Alert() component can embed a Badge() for the severity label, keeping both independently reusable.

#' An Alert
#'
#' @param message String /// Required.
#'                Message to show on the alert.
#'
#' @param type String /// Optional.
#'                Alert type. A utility class. Must be one of:
#'                - "info" (default)
#'                - "danger"
#'                - "warning"
#'
#' @param dismissible String /// Optional.
#'                 Should the alert be dismissible? Either:
#'                 - "no" (default)
#'                 - "yes"
#'
#' @return `hypertext.tag`
#'
#' @export
Alert <- function(
  message,
  type = c("info", "danger", "warning"),
  dismissible = c("no", "yes")
) {
  type <- match.arg(arg = type)
  dismissible <- match.arg(arg = dismissible)

  label <- switch(
    EXPR = type,
    info = Badge(label = "info", variant = "success"),
    warning = Badge(label = "warning", variant = "primary"),
    danger = Badge(label = "danger", variant = "danger")
  )

  classes <- c("alert", paste0("alert-", type))
  dismiss_btn <- NULL
  if (dismissible == "yes") {
    classes <- c(
      classes,
      "alert-dismissible fade show"
    )

    dismiss_btn <- tags$button(
      type = "button",
      class = "btn-close",
      `data-bs-dismiss` = "alert",
      `aria-label` = "Close"
    )
  }

  tags$div(
    class = classes,
    role = "alert",
    label,
    " ",
    message,
    dismiss_btn
  )
}

alert_a <- Alert(
  message = "Your session will expire in 5 minutes.",
  type = "warning"
)

alert_b <- Alert(
  message = "Record deleted successfully.",
  type = "danger"
)

alert_c <- Alert(
  message = "This is some info you need to know.",
  dismissible = "yes"
)
cat(
  render(alert_a),
  render(alert_b),
  render(alert_c)
)
warning Your session will expire in 5 minutes.
danger Record deleted successfully.
info This is some info you need to know.

Conditional children

Use NULL as a sentinel for optional children. render() drops NULL values silently, so you can conditionally include children without special-casing the rendering logic.

card <- function(
  title,
  body,
  footer = NULL
) {
  tags$div(
    class = "card",
    tags$div(
      class = "card-header",
      tags$strong(title)
    ),
    tags$div(
      class = "card-body",
      tags$p(
        class = "card-text",
        body
      )
    ),
    if (!is.null(footer)) {
      tags$div(
        class = "card-footer text-muted",
        footer
      )
    }
  )
}

# without footer
card_a <- card(
  title = "Title A",
  body = "Some body text."
)
# with footer
card_b <- card(
  title = "Title B",
  body = "Some body text.",
  footer = "Last updated: today"
)
cat(
render(card_a),
render(card_b)
)
Title A

Some body text.

Title B

Some body text.

Last updated: today

When footer is NULL the if expression evaluates to NULL, which hypertext simply ignores. No conditional rendering plumbing needed.

ellipsis

sometimes, you need to pass an unknown number of tags as children to a tag. that’s where the dots (...) come in.

List children

lapply() returns a plain list, and hypertext flattens plain lists of children automatically. This means you can map over a vector to produce a list of child nodes without any extra unwrapping.

nav_bar <- function(links) {
  # links: named character vector, names = labels, values = hrefs
  items <- lapply(
    X = names(links),
    FUN = function(label) {
      tags$li(
        class = "nav-item",
        tags$a(
          class = "nav-link",
          href = links[[label]],
          label
        )
      )
    }
  )

  tags$nav(
    tags$ul(
      class = "nav",
      items
    )
  )
}

site_links <- c(
  "Home" = "/",
  "Reference" = "/reference/",
  "Articles" = "/articles/"
)

my_nav <- nav_bar(site_links)
cat(
  render(my_nav)
)
  • Home
  • Reference
  • Articles

Worked example — profile card

Putting it together: a profile_card() component that combines an image, a heading, a paragraph, and the Badge() component from earlier.

profile_card <- function(name, role, bio, avatar_url) {
  tags$div(
    class = "card",
    style = "max-width: 320px;",
    tags$img(
      src = avatar_url,
      class = "card-img-top",
      alt = paste("Photo of", name)
    ),
    tags$div(
      class = "card-body",
      tags$h5(class = "card-title", name),
      Badge(role, variant = "success"),
      tags$p(class = "card-text mt-2", bio)
    )
  )
}

my_profile <- profile_card(
  name = "Ada Lovelace",
  role = "mathematician",
  bio = paste(
    "First to recognise that a computing machine had",
    "applications beyond pure calculation."
  ),
  avatar_url = "https://example.com/ada.jpg"
)
cat(
  render(my_profile)
)
Ada Lovelace
mathematician

First to recognise that a computing machine had applications beyond pure calculation.

Summary

Pattern How
Fixed structure, variable content Function argument → attribute or child
Optional children Default to NULL; if (!is.null(x)) guard
Composition Call one component function inside another
Lists of children lapply() → plain list → auto-flattened
Reuse across pages Source the file or put components in a package

A component library for a real project is just a collection of these functions. There is no registration, no lifecycle, no virtual DOM — just R functions that return tag objects.

On this page

Developed by Kennedy Mwavu, Sigflux.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
